relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: The chapter turns war into a geographic register of defeated rulers.
Sihon's territory is mapped from Arnon toward Jabbok and Gilead.
Arabah, Chinneroth, Salt Sea, and Pisgah complete the eastern boundary.
Og is remembered as a remaining Rephaim king at Ashtaroth and Edrei.
Hermon, Salecah, Bashan, and Gilead define his domain.
Moses' victories become inheritance for Reuben, Gad, and half Manasseh.
Joshua's western victories are mapped from Lebanon's valley toward Seir.
Hill, lowland, wilderness, and southland become categories of possession.
Jericho and Ai are reduced to one king each.
Jerusalem and Hebron become two more counted outcomes.
Jarmuth and Lachish enter the list.
Eglon and Gezer follow.
Debir and Geder are counted.
Hormah and Arad are counted.
Libnah and Adullam are counted.
Makkedah and Bethel are counted.
Tappuah and Hepher are counted.
Aphek and Lasharon are counted.
Madon and Hazor are counted.
Shimron-meron and Achshaph are counted.
Taanach and Megiddo are counted.
Kedesh and Jokneam are counted.
Dor and Goiim in Gilgal are counted.
Tirzah completes the total of thirty-one kings.

contemplative_note: Joshua 12 demonstrates how administration can make violence look clean. A city becomes a king, a campaign becomes a number, and a people disappears into a list. Ethical memory must restore the human lives that victory records omit.
